posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked:
Does the site upgrade mean my notes are better, too?
Couple from this weekend.

  • 2011 Morey-Blanc Meursault 1er Cru Charmes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Meursault 1er Cru (8/12/2018)
    So much more giving than my last bottle. Opened and let sit for 30 mins. The acid that was so present in the last bottle is here, but it is buffered by honey, sweet lemon, and cool, steely minerality. It is still youthful, but certiainly more giving than two years ago. [/i]
  • 2010 Claude Dugat Gevrey-Chambertin - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Gevrey-Chambertin (8/11/2018)
    Dark and brooding in the glass. Modern, new world-ish pinot. This is what Dugat wines are to me. That and way over-priced, but that’s another story. Underbrush and some appealing tertiary scents. High gloss, clean and pure, but ripe fruit. Turns iron-tart on the finish. It is a sound, enjoyable, albeit over-priced pinot noir that is drinking fine just now.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked: YOUR NOTES ARE 100PTS NOW!

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.cooked: I am always surprised that the wines of Claude Dugat are considered as new world pinot noir. When you visit there is absolutely nothing modern about the winery. Makes you wonder whether the lighter style currently en vogue is not the more worked one. And his wines are definitely on the cheaper end of the spectrum ex-domaine.

posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked: Interesting take JW. I’ve only had a handful, from villages to Lavaux and some others. With enough age, it would seem, but nothing that I would consider “older” Maybe I’m catching them too young. Always strike me as full-fruit and rather polished/modern.

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.cooked:
Interesting take JW. I’ve only had a handful, from villages to Lavaux and some others. With enough age, it would seem, but nothing that I would consider “older” Maybe I’m catching them too young. Always strike me as full-fruit and rather polished/modern.

Just my personal view, Dennis, from visiting Burgundy once in a while. Although I do wonder what cellar practices make them taste “polished/modern” to you - would be an interesting discussion and comparison, albeit one that is unlikely to happen…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think the two elements of Claude Dugat’s approach that could be described as “modern” were (I use the past tense because his children are now directing the estate and have made subtle changes) his use of large percentages of new oak and his practice of avoiding (if I am not mistaken) racking his wines of their original lees until their assembly in tank for bottling.

In all other respect, Dugat’s winemaking was extremely traditional. In the vineyard, old selection massale plants, meticulous viticulture (like his cousin’s vines, Dugat’s vines are immediately recognizable by virtue of the quality of the farming) and moderate yields. In the cuverie and cellar, 100% destemming (for which there is in fact a long tradition in Burgundy - it being done by hand with wooden grills back in the day), fermentation without a cold soak in cement tanks, often with light chaptalization (something the children have stopped), twice-daily punch downs, two winters’ élevage and bottling without fining and filtration.

The wines and the family are warmly respected in France and within Burgundy, and I have always wondered why they were controversial in the USA. Did their former importer, Kacher, pick the most overtly oaky barrels for export? Did the critical acclaim they received from some quarters provoke a reaction in others? Or is it simply that the wines need quite a bit more bottle age than many of their neighbors’? Perhaps it’s simply a divisive style to the Anglo-Saxon palate. What I can say is that you’d have derived a lot more pleasure, Dennis, from the 2011 right now than the 2010. In terms of ‘top vintages’, the 2002 is the youngest that is really drinking at its best.

I recently drank a bottle of Claude Dugat over lunch with Jean-François Coche, and he pronounced it ‘excellent: the archetype of Gevrey-Chambertin’. One man’s opinion, but one that I’d second.

Also, as JW notes, the prices are some of the region’s most reasonable: the village Gevrey is 30-35 EUR from the Domaine.

posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked:
Thanks, guys, for the ongoing thoughts. William, great info. you are a helluva lot more knowledgeable here than most, I appreciate the details.
I will say I was not cognizant of Dugat as controversial, and wasn’t leading/feeding that with my note. I just happened on some at a decent price and took a spin.
What’s the knock? The oak and modern feel?
All that being said, this is a $100 village wine in US dollars. It doesn’t get there for me. Perhaps it needed 10 more years.
Thanks again for the discussion.

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.cooked:
Did their former importer, Kacher, pick the most overtly oaky barrels for export?

I do think that this is an interesting point, although the only winemaker I know whose importer selects barrels specifically for the American market, is Gerard Raphet. So there, the wines for the US market are definitely not the same as for the European market. I would be surprised that someone like Claude Dugat would agree to this, but you never know. I’ll be in Burgundy next week, so will try to ask.

Indeed an interesting discussion, and William clearly knows a lot more about all the ins and out than I do, so thanks for the insights.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Did their former importer, Kacher, pick the most overtly oaky barrels for export?

I do think that this is an interesting point, although the only winemaker I know whose importer selects barrels specifically for the American market, is Gerard Raphet. So there, the wines for the US market are definitely not the same as for the European market. I would be surprised that someone like Claude Dugat would agree to this, but you never know. I’ll be in Burgundy next week, so will try to ask.

Indeed an interesting discussion, and William clearly knows a lot more about all the ins and out than I do, so thanks for the insights.

It is not uncommon for different importers to demand different products - filtered or unfiltered, different barrel selections etc.

Did the critical acclaim they received from some quarters provoke a reaction in others? Or is it simply that the wines need quite a bit more bottle age than many of their neighbors’?

IMO, it’s some of both. The Dugat wines drew lavish praise from voices that didn’t always speak to Burgundy lovers, and so there was pushback against that. I saw that happen to multiple producers whose style evolved from the loathed “over-oaked and over-extracted” late-90s to more nuanced expressions in the 00s. To be fair, Kacher tended to err on the side of oak, and some of his critically well-received producers like Bertrand Armboise did tend to oak everything too much.

That said, a 96 Dugat Gevrey (which was expensive on release) showed like a pretty classic Gevrey about 15 years. I always think of Dominque Laurent as the classic example of someone whose wines everyone hated young despite praise from critics who said “wait” and then loved when mature.

Dugat-Py is another example of a controversial producer. I only have experience from 05 forward, and I can say that wines that showed crazy young have really blossomed at 10 years, albeit with darker fruit and a producer signature.
